coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon dots from maleic anhydride and tetraethylenepentamine via pyrolysis for sensing, adsorbance, and imaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Thongsai, Nichaphat
Nagae, Yusuke
Hirai, Tomoyasu
Takahara, Atsushi
Uchiyama, Tomoki
Kamitani, Kazutaka
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are materials with fascinating optical and electronic properties that can replace organic dyes and fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals. In this work, we developed a one-step pyrolysis for producing fluorescent, water-soluble, and highly nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) from the readily available chemical precursors: tetraethylenepentamine and maleic anhydride. NCDs exhibited blue fluorescent emission with a relatively high quantum yield of 21% and average diameter of approximately 8nm. These NCDs were found to be selective sensors for Cu2+, with a limit of detection of 0.62μM. A simple NCD-coated, paper-based sensor demonstrated that the NCDs were highly responsive to and selective of Cu2+. Furthermore, X-ray absorption fine structure measurements showed the unique coordinating properties of the NCDs. The copper complex changed from a distorted octahedral to a square planar structure upon the addition of NCDs. The NCDs were also found to be an excellent adsorbent for Cu2+, with a surface coverage of 1.5 ions nm−2, equivalent to 214mgg−1 adsorption capacity. In addition, NCDs were used to image a human fingerprint and for the preparation of fluorescent plastics. These results demonstrated that the multifunctional NCDs with excellent optical properties and water solubility produced in this work can be used in a range of applications, including sensors, adsorbents, imaging, and forensic science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sensor
Adsorbent
Fingerprint imaging
Fluorescent plastic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Metal ion sensing and adsorption of NCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis and applications used in this work.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of NCDs, (b) Survey XPS spectrum of NCDs, and the high resolution of (c) C1s, (d) N1s, and (e) O1s peaks.
(a) TEM image and (b) the size distribution of NCDs from DLS.

(a) UV–vis spectrum, (b) fluorescent spectra at different excitation wavelengths. The inset in (b) shows figures for NCD solutions under visible (left) and UV light (right), and (c) fluorescent spectra at different pH values using an excitation wavelength of 360nm.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs in the presence of different metal ions, (b) relative fluorescent intensity of NCDs with and without metal ions, (c) fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of Cu2+ at various concentrations, (d) fluorescent emission intensity of NCDs in the presence of Cu2+ at various concentrations. The inset plot shows fluorescent detection of Cu2+ fitted into Stern–Volmer equation where F0 and F are the fluorescent intensities in the absence and presence of Cu2+, (e) photograph of NCD-coated filter paper with metal ions under UV illumination, and (f) photograph of NCD-coated filter paper with Cu2+ at different concentrations under UV illumination.
(a) XANES spectra, and (b) change of copper complex structure from distorted octahedral to square planar.
(a) Human fingerprint on glass slide detected with NCD solution under UV light and (b) fluorescent plastics from PMMA and NCDs.
Comparison of sensing performance of different fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection.
Comparison of adsorption capacities of different adsorbents for Cu2+ ions.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon dots from maleic anhydride and tetraethylenepentamine via pyrolysis for sensing, adsorbance, and imaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Materials Chemistry and Engineering, Kyushu University, Fukuoka, 819-0395, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Materials Chemistry and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Japan Synchrotron Radiation Research Institute (JASRI), Hyogo 679-5198, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are materials with fascinating optical and electronic properties that can replace organic dyes and fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals. In this work, we developed a one-step pyrolysis for producing fluorescent, water-soluble, and highly nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) from the readily available chemical precursors: tetraethylenepentamine and maleic anhydride. NCDs exhibited blue fluorescent emission with a relatively high quantum yield of 21% and average diameter of approximately 8nm. These NCDs were found to be selective sensors for Cu2+, with a limit of detection of 0.62μM. A simple NCD-coated, paper-based sensor demonstrated that the NCDs were highly responsive to and selective of Cu2+. Furthermore, X-ray absorption fine structure measurements showed the unique coordinating properties of the NCDs. The copper complex changed from a distorted octahedral to a square planar structure upon the addition of NCDs. The NCDs were also found to be an excellent adsorbent for Cu2+, with a surface coverage of 1.5 ions nm−2, equivalent to 214mgg−1 adsorption capacity. In addition, NCDs were used to image a human fingerprint and for the preparation of fluorescent plastics. These results demonstrated that the multifunctional NCDs with excellent optical properties and water solubility produced in this work can be used in a range of applications, including sensors, adsorbents, imaging, and forensic science.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials used in diverse applications, for example, bio-imaging probes, drug and gene delivery, sensors, fluorescent ink, optoelectronic devices, and photocatalysis [1–4]. In 2004, Xu et al. discovered CDs during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [5]. They have been considered superior fluorescent materials to conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in terms of high water-solubility, stable photoluminescence, chemical inertness, tunable excitation, low toxicity, anti-oxidant activity, and easy functionalization [6–9]. In addition, CDs can be prepared using several methods from readily available raw materials, thereby providing ease of tunability, processability, diversity, and scaling-up potentials.

CDs can be prepared by top-down and bottom-up routes. In the top-down route, CDs are formed by breaking down bulk carbon-rich materials like graphite [10], graphite oxide [11], carbon nanotubes [12], carbon soot [13], and activated carbon [14] using laser ablation [15,16], arc discharge [5], and electrochemical oxidation [17]. These methods, however, require high-energy sources and generally give low product yields. The bottom-up approach generally involves hydrolysis, polymerization, and carbonization reactions of small molecules and polymers under thermal treatment. A range of carbon-containing materials, including the natural precursors [18–22], chemical precursors [17,23–27], and wastes [28–30], have been used to synthesize CDs. In addition, the ease of synthesis and quality of products make the bottom-up approach even more attractive. Different thermal processes have been employed, including pyrolysis/combustion [25], solvothermal [31], hydrothermal [32], and microwave techniques [33]. These methods use milder conditions and give higher yields than the top-down methods. However, CDs produced by both top-down and bottom-up methods typically have lower quantum yields than semiconductor quantum dots. Recently, chemical doping of CDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen [27,34–40] and sulfur [38–41], and post-functionalization of CDs with organic amine compounds such as diamine-terminated oligomeric polyethylene glycol, have been found to improve quantum yields [10]. It has been proposed that the heteroatoms act as emissive trap sites for photoexcited electrons, enhancing the optical properties [42].

In this work, we proposed a simple, one-step synthesis of multifunctional nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) from commercially available maleic anhydride and tetraethylenepentamine, an amine-rich, polymerizable compound, using pyrolysis. This is considered one of the most convenient and simple methods for the preparation of CDs. Recently, Niu et al. reported the use of ethylenediamine to produce NCDs with quantum efficiencies as high as 46.2% [43]. To the best of our knowledge, no previous study has used a precursor like tetraethylenepentamine which has five amine groups per molecule. The NCDs prepared in this work were characterized using UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), zeta potential measurement, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). NCDs were investigated in applications including metal ion detection, fingerprint imaging, and the preparation of fluorescent plastics. This was done to investigate the versatility of NCDs. We further analyzed the change of complex structure upon adsorption of Cu2+ on NCDs, using X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) and extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) measurements. The results suggest that the NCDs developed in this work will have many applications, including sensing, imaging, and forensic work.

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification unless otherwise noted. Maleic anhydride (MA), tetraethylenepentamine (TEP), and quinine sulfate were purchased from Acros Organics. Sulfuric acid (96% w/w) was obtained from Carlo Erba. High-grade regenerated cellulose dialysis membrane (1000Da MWCO) was bought from Membrane Filtration Product, Inc. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments.

MA (0.5g), TEP (10mL), 0.5M H2SO4 (10mL), and DI water (10mL) were placed in a porcelain crucible and pyrolyzed at 250°C for 6h in a furnace. The reaction mixture was then cooled to room temperature, diluted with DI water (100mL), and filtered using a cellulose filter paper. The filtrate was neutralized using 0.5M H2SO4. The solution was then centrifuged at 10000rpm for 15min. The supernatant was purified using dialysis membrane (1000Da MWCO). Finally, the solution was freeze dried to yield a brown solid (0.0788g).

The relative quantum yields of the NCDs were measured using the following equation [44,45]:
Φ =
Φ
R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n
2
n R
2
where ϕ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity measured using a fluorescent spectrometer, n is the refractive index, A is the absorbance measured using a UV–vis spectrometer, and the subscript R indicates the reference. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 was used as a reference (ϕR =54% at 360nm).

Co2+ (CoCl2·6H2O), Mg2+ (MgCl2), Sn2+ (SnCl2), Fe2+ (Fe(NH4)2(SO4)2), Cu2+(CuSO4·5H2O), Pb2+ (PbCl2), Zn2+ (ZnCl2), Na+ (NaNO3), Fe3+ (FeCl3), Ni2+ (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), and Ag+ (AgNO3) ions were chosen to test their selectivity with NCDs. Metal ion solutions (1.5mL, 50μM) and NCD solution (3.5mL, 0.02gL−1) were mixed and transferred to a quartz cuvette. The fluorescent emission spectra were then recorded.

A human fingerprint was placed on a glass slide. NCD solution (0.05gL−1) was then dropped and spread on the fingerprint and allowed to air-dry. The NCD-covered fingerprint was then imaged under UV illumination.

Poly(methylmethacrylate) (PMMA) and NCDs were used to prepare fluorescent plastics. PMMA (20g) in acetone (50mL) and NCD (0.25g) in DI water (50mL) were mixed and poured into a glass mold. The mixture was then allowed to air-dry at room temperature (30°C).

The FT-IR spectra were obtained using a Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer (Spectrum 2000) and KBr pellets. The surface elements of the sample were investigated using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos analytical, Manchester UK). The base pressure in the analysis chamber was about 5×10−9 torr. The solution of samples was dropped on 1×1cm2 glass slide, and were excited with X-ray hybrid mode at 700×300cm2 spot area with monochromatic Al K α
1,2 radiations at 1.4 keV. The X-ray anode was run at 15kV, 10mA (150W). The photoelectrons were detected with a hemispherical analyzer positioned at an angle of 45° with respect to the normal to the sample surface. The TEM image was obtained using JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope. The accelerating voltage was 200kV and the magnification was 800,000X. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectra of NCD aqueous solution (0.02gL−1) were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-1700 PharmaSpec), and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jasco, FP-6200), respectively. The size and zeta potential of the NCD solutions (0.01gL−1) were measured using a Nano Particle Analyzer (HORIBA, Nano Partica SZ-100) at 25°C with scattering angle of 173°. Refractive index, viscosity, pH, and conductivity of solution are 1.33, 0.894mPas, 12, and 31.327mScm−1, respectively. The electrode voltage for zeta potential measurement was 0.9V. X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS) measurements at Cu K-edge (8.98 keV) were performed at Kyushu University beamline (BL06/SAGA-LS). The synchrotron radiations were monochromated by a Si (111) double crystal monochromator. Energy calibration was carried out using Cu-foil as a standard. Rhodium-coated mirror was used to eliminate higher harmonics. The measurements were performed at room temperature in transmission mode using a pair of ionization chambers for monitoring incident and transmitted X-ray intensities. The sample of Cu aqueous solution was fixed in a polyethylene bag to adjust the thickness. The data analysis was processed using ATHENA and ARTEMIS, a suite of IFEFFIT software programs [46]. EXAFS oscillations, χ (k), were extracted from the data using a Victoreen polynomial function and normalized to the edge height. The k3-weighted EXAFS data (k3χ(k)) was Fourier transformed to R-space over 3.0–11.5Å−1 using a Hanning window function.We conducted q-space fitting using back Fourier transformed EXAFS with R range of 1.2–2.7Å.

Water-soluble MA and TEP were chosen as the carbon and nitrogen sources for the synthesis of the NCDs. The prepared NCDs were then used in applications including metal ion sensing, fingerprint imaging, and fluorescent plastics, to test their versatility (Fig. 1 ). MA is a commonly used monomer for the synthesis of polyesters and other functional polymers, while TEP is an amine-rich molecule widely used as a ligand when forming complexes with transition metals. Amine-rich molecules have been reported to produce high quantum efficiencies as the carbon and nitrogen atoms incorporate into the framework of the NCDs, creating favorable emissive states [27,34–40]. Upon pyrolysis in the presence of sulfuric acid at 250°C for 6h, MA and TEP were polymerized via a step reaction and then carbonized to form NCDs. These reaction conditions were found to be optimum because other temperatures and reaction times produced much lower yields of carbon dots.

Surface functional groups and composition of the NCDs were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum showed OH stretching, CO stretching, CC stretching, and CN/CO stretching vibrations at 3393, 1647, 1447, and 1100cm−1, respectively (Fig. 2 a). Strong OH and CO signals suggested the presence of hydroxyl and carboxylate/carbonyl functional groups, which made the NCDs highly soluble in water. The survey XPS spectrum showed signals of O1s, N1s, C1s, and S2p at 531.5eV, 399.5, 285.5eV, and 167.5eV, respectively (Fig. 2b). The presence of sulfur atoms was due to the use of sulfuric acid as catalyst. The atomic concentrations of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur were found to be 66.26%, 14.26%, 13.46%, and 0.48%, respectively. The high concentration of nitrogen indicated that the NCDs were strongly nitrogen-doped. The high resolution C1s peak showed predominant graphitic, nitrogenated, and oxygenated carbons at 284.8eV, 285.7eV, and 286.5-288.6eV, respectively (Fig. 2c). The high resolution of the N1s peak showed the presence of pyridinic, pyrrolinic, and graphitic nitrogens at 399.7eV, 400.5eV, and 401.5eV (Fig. 2d). In addition, the high resolution of the O1s peak confirmed the existence of CO, CO, and OCO at 531.4eV, 532.2eV, and 533.6eV (Fig. 2e), which is consistent with the FT-IR result and the C1s peak [47]. Both the FT-IR and XPS results confirmed the presence of hydrophilic oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of the NCDs. Signals at 152.5eV belonged to the silicon substrate used for sample preparation.

The morphology and size of the NCDs were characterized using TEM and DLS. The TEM images of the NCDs showed spherical shapes with a diameter of about 20nm and a lattice spacing of 0.32nm, close to that of graphite (Fig. 3 a) [48]. This indicated the presence of graphitic carbon in the NCDs, consistent with the UV–vis and XPS results. The size distribution from DLS showed an average diameter of 8.0nm (Fig. 3b). The large size and broad distribution indicated the formation of aggregates due to the highly hydrophilic nature of the NCDs, as previously reported [3]. The zeta potential of the NCDs was found to be −2.7mV, indicating a slightly negative surface and aggregation of the NCDs, in good agreement with the TEM and DLS results.

The absorption and emission properties of the NCDs in aqueous solution were characterized using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, respectively. The absorption spectrum showed a broad absorption band around 200–300nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 carbons in multiple-aromatic chromophores (Fig. 4 a) [49,50]. The shoulder peak at about 300–400nm belonged to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl groups [51]. These results confirmed that the NCDs contained the aromatic and oxygen-containing groups. The long tail absorption in the visible region was attributed to distribution of different-sized particles. The fluorescent spectra showed the strongest emission at 450nm using an excitation wavelength of 360nm (Fig. 4b). The fluorescent emission was attributed to the radiative recombination between electrons and holes which are trapped at the surface sites from the photoinduced charge separation [52]. The emission was found to be excitation-dependent, suggesting that the fluorescent emission originated from different emissive sites and mechanisms [2]. The NCD solution showed blue emission under UV irradiation at a wavelength of 254nm. In addition, pH-dependent fluorescent emission was observed (Fig. 4c). The NCDs showed high fluorescent intensity in acidic solution, followed by neutral and basic solutions, as previously reported [53,54]. This pH-dependent fluorescent behavior was believed to arise from the protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups, leading to electrostatic doping and charging of the NCDs [55]. The quantum yield of the NCDs was 21%, using quinine sulfate as a reference, which is relatively high compared to results from previous studies [56,57]. The high quantum yield was possibly due to the formation of favorable emissive states because of the presence of nitrogen-rich groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing and adsorption of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: We also explored the adsorbent properties of the NCDs. We found that the fluorescent intensity reduced as the concentration of Cu2+ was increased from 0 to 100μM, becoming constant when the concentration exceeded 100μM (Fig. 5d). This suggested that the fluorescent quenching of the NCDs by the addition of Cu2+ reached equilibrium or saturation at 100μM. A Cu2+ concentration of 100μM with respect to 0.05gL−1 NCD is equivalent to an adsorption capacity of approximately 214mgg−1. The Cu2+ adsorption capacity of the NCDs was higher than that of other materials possibly due to their high surface-to-volume ratio of nanosized NCDs and their strong affinity toward Cu2+ ions (Table 2 ). Our NCDs therefore provide an excellent adsorbent for Cu2+ ions. Since the diameter of the NCDs from the DLS results was approximately 8nm, the 214mgg−1 adsorption capacity was equivalent to 1.6 ions nm−2 (calculation in supplementary information). Based on these results, the NCDs developed in this work can be used as both a selective and responsive sensor and high adsorption capacity adsorbent for the detection and removal of Cu2+ ions. This is important as Cu2+ ions are highly toxic to living organisms [58,67].

To gain further insights into the interaction between NCDs and Cu2+, a mixture of NCDs and Cu2+ in solution was investigated using XANES. The ratio of NCDs to Cu2+ was varied. A shoulder signal at 8985eV was observed at ratios of 1:5 and 1:10 Cu2+:NCD, corresponding to a square planar structure (Fig. 6 a) [71]. This suggests that the copper complex changed from a distorted octahedral to a square planar structure as the concentration of NCDs was increased [72]. The EXAFS results showed that the Cu-OSO3 bond length increased from 2.2 to 2.9Å as the concentration of NCDs was increased (Table S1). This increase in bond length suggested a loss of sulfate group and water molecule along the z-axis upon the addition of NCDs, and the consequent change in structure of the copper complex (Fig. 6b). A concentration ratio of 1:5 Cu2+:NCD, at which the formation of square planar complex formed, is equivalent to approximately 1.5 ions nm−2. Interestingly, this value is close to that derived from the fluorescent quenching experiment. These results suggest the correlation between fluorescent quenching and the change in complex structure. We exclude a coordination effect between NCDs and sulfate group because Fe(NH4)2(SO4)2, which was also used in this work, did not quench the fluorescence of NCDs. The loss of sulfate group and water molecule and the change in complex structure may arise from strong affinity between NCDs and Cu2+. Despite rarity, square-planar copper complexes have been prepared from sterically hindered and multi-dentate ligands [73,74]. These results suggest that NCDs can act as unique ligands for the preparation of novel metal-organic complexes for catalysis and other applications.

The NCDs developed in this work were also used for fingerprint imaging. Fingerprint analysis has been used to identify suspects and solve crimes. Fluorescent dyes such as ninhydrin, indocyanine green, and fluorescein have been used, but these dyes are toxic and relatively expensive. NCDs can provide an alternative. We found that a fingerprint could be detected using our NCDs (Fig. 7 a). Therefore, the non-toxic, easy-to-use, and water-soluble NCDs developed in this work can be potentially be used for fingerprint detection and imaging, which will be particularly useful in the field of forensic science.

Fluorescent plastics are used in toys, paints, and banknotes. In this work, NCDs were used to prepare fluorescent plastics. PMMA was chosen because it is a glassy, transparent polymer used in many applications. PMMA was mixed with NCDs and cast as a film. The NCD/PMMA composite showed blue emission under UV light (Fig. 7b). The film was flexible and could be cut into various shapes and sizes. The fluorescent emission in a solid polymer composite is crucial for the fabrication of flexible, large-scale optoelectronic devices [75].

This work demonstrated that highly fluorescent, water-soluble, and nitrogen-doped NCDs could be produced from commercially available tetraethylenepentamine and maleic anhydride using a one-step pyrolysis method. The NCDs exhibited blue emission with a high quantum yield of 21%. The NCDs showed excellent selectivity, sensitivity, and adsorption of Cu2+. In addition, NCDs were found to have a unique coordinating ability that could change the complex structure, as confirmed by XANES and EXAFS. The NCDs were used for fingerprint imaging and the preparation of fluorescent plastics. The multifunctional NCDs developed in this work have many applications, including sensors, adsorbents, imaging, and forensic science. Future work includes the incorporation of NCDs with solid support or magnetic nanoparticles and the improvement of these of paper-based NCD devices by increasing their sensitivity and accuracy, allowing them for practical uses. Further investigation and analysis of the interaction between Cu2+ and NCDs can be carried out using X-ray and crystallographic techniques.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University (Contract Number (2) 5/2559). The authors also acknowledge the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC), Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University. N. Thongsai is funded by the Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand. The part of this work was supported by Photon and Quantum Basic Research Coordinated Development Program for the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology, Japan.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.07.051.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Nichaphat Thongsai studies in the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University as a Ph.D. graduate student.
Yusuke Nagae received his Bachelor and Master degrees from Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyushu University.

Tomoyasu Hirai is an assistant professor at Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyushu University. He received his Bachelor of Engineering degree from Gunma University, and Master and Doctor of Engineering degrees from Tokyo Institute of Technology. His research interests are polymer synthesis, and polymer structure, properties and applications.
Atsushi Takahara is a professor at Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyushu University. He received his Ph.D. degree (Engineering) from Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyushu University.

Tomoki Uchiyama received his Ph.D. degree of Engineering from Kyushu University in 2015. Currently, he works for JASRI (Japan Synchrotron Radiation Research Institute) at SPring-8 as a post-doctoral position. His scientific interests focused on catalysts, inorganic materials, and synchrotron X-ray analysis.

Kazutaka Kamitani received his Ph.D. degree in Material Engineering from Nagasaki University of Science and Technology in 2010. He then joined Kyushu University, where currently he is a Postdoctoral Researcher in Institute for Materials Chemistry and Engineering. His current scientific interests focused on nanomaterials, ceramics, and analytical chemistry.

Peerasak Paoprasert is currently an assistant professor at Thammasat University. He received his B.A. from Northwestern University in 2005 and Ph.D. from University of Wisconsin-Madison in 2010. He joined Thammasat University in 2011 and has been working on the design and synthesis of functional materials.
